summary: This fictional narrative follows Jonathan, a brilliant quantitative trader at Operant Capital, who has been leading the RPS (rock-paper-scissors) project—an attempt to predict human choices before they're made. The research builds on neuroscience findings that brain scans can detect decisions before conscious awareness. After years of incremental progress reaching 54% first-round accuracy, Jonathan returns from a weekend away to discover his team has achieved something far more disturbing: perfect prediction of his entire sequence of moves, including when he would leave the room, all computed before he played a single game.
key_points: Markets are fundamentally different from physics—they're emergent behavior from billions of agents predicting each other's predictions
The RPS project explores whether human decisions can be predicted from external signals like social media posts or facial features
Early neuroscience research showed MRI machines could detect button-press decisions before subjects were consciously aware of their choice
The breakthrough isn't just prediction—it's that feedback becomes unnecessary when predictions are known to be correct
The story raises questions about free will and determinism through a near-future financial technology lens
money_quotes: The market moves based on the thoughts and feelings of billions of humans, and they were all trying to do this same prediction thing.
This was a Red Queen's race, where the tactics that worked last year didn't work this year. Everyone was predicting everyone else predicting the market.
Technical analysis is complete bullshit. This is a simple betting game played against other agents.
But what was crazier is that the machine knew which button you would press before you were even aware of your choice.
Maybe you had to look inside to spot a scissors type guy.
If it knows all the predictions are going to be correct, why does it need any feedback?
key_insight: If prediction becomes perfect, the distinction between foreknowledge and determinism collapses—free will becomes an illusion we haven't yet instrumented away.
